cards.title: REST Resource Design & HTTP Semantics
cards.explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style where resources are identified by URIs and manipulated via a uniform interface. Resources should be nouns, not verbs — /orders, not /createOrder. HTTP methods carry semantic meaning: GET retrieves (safe, idempotent), POST creates or triggers (neither), PUT replaces a resource (idempotent), PATCH partially updates (idempotent if designed correctly), DELETE removes (idempotent). Status codes communicate outcome: 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
cards.codeExample:
// Resource naming — nouns, hierarchical
GET    /users              → list users
GET    /users/42           → get user 42
POST   /users              → create a user
PUT    /users/42           → replace user 42
PATCH  /users/42           → partial update
DELETE /users/42           → delete user 42
GET    /users/42/orders    → orders belonging to user 42

// Status codes
200 OK             — success with body
201 Created        — POST success; include Location: /users/42 header
204 No Content     — success, no body (DELETE, PUT)
400 Bad Request    — invalid input (include error details in body)
401 Unauthorized   — not authenticated
403 Forbidden      — authenticated but not authorised
404 Not Found      — resource doesn't exist
409 Conflict       — e.g. duplicate unique field
422 Unprocessable  — valid JSON but fails business validation
429 Too Many Req.  — rate limited; include Retry-After header
500 Internal Error — never leak stack traces in production
cards.whyItMatters: Consistent resource naming and correct status codes make APIs predictable. Clients can infer behaviour from conventions without reading docs. 4xx vs 5xx distinctions tell the caller whether retrying makes sense.
cards.gotcha: Using POST for everything (RPC-style) is a common REST anti-pattern. It loses HTTP cache semantics, breaks idempotency, and makes API behaviour unpredictable. Reserve POST for non-idempotent creation or actions; use PUT/PATCH for updates.

cards.title: Idempotency & Safety
cards.explanation: A safe method has no side effects (GET, HEAD). An idempotent method produces the same server state whether called once or N times (GET, PUT, DELETE — and POST with an idempotency key). Idempotency is critical for retry logic: if a payment POST fails mid-flight, the client shouldn't know whether to retry. The solution is an idempotency key (a UUID sent in a header); the server stores the result and returns it on duplicate requests, preventing double charges.
cards.whyItMatters: Network failures happen. Without idempotency keys, every retry risks duplicate side effects — double charges, duplicate emails, duplicate records. Idempotency keys are standard practice for any API that performs financial transactions or sends communications.
cards.gotcha: PUT is idempotent by definition but requires the client to send the complete resource. This means a client that doesn't know all fields can accidentally erase them. PATCH is safer for partial updates — but PATCH idempotency must be implemented explicitly (e.g. using conditional ETags).

cards.title: API Versioning Strategies
cards.explanation: When a breaking change is required, clients must not be forced to upgrade immediately. Three main versioning strategies: URI versioning (/v1/users) is the most explicit and cacheable; header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v2+json) keeps URIs clean but is invisible in the browser; query parameter versioning (/users?version=2) is simple but clutters query strings. URI versioning is the most common in practice. Semantic versioning applies to libraries; APIs typically use major-version-only in the URI.
cards.whyItMatters: APIs are contracts. Breaking changes without versioning force all clients to update simultaneously — impossible in practice. A clear versioning policy lets teams evolve APIs independently while keeping existing integrations working.
cards.gotcha: Maintaining multiple API versions in parallel is expensive. Set a deprecation timeline (e.g. v1 sunset in 6 months after v2 launch), communicate it via a Deprecation header and email, and actively track which clients are still on the old version.

cards.title: gRPC Basics
cards.explanation: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (a binary serialization format) and HTTP/2. You define services and messages in a .proto file, and the gRPC toolchain generates type-safe client and server stubs in any language. It supports four call types: unary (one request, one response), server streaming, client streaming, and bidirectional streaming. gRPC is preferred over REST for internal service-to-service communication where performance matters and both sides are under your control.
cards.whyItMatters: gRPC payloads are typically 5–10× smaller than equivalent JSON and 2–3× faster to parse. It enforces a contract via the .proto schema, making breaking changes visible at compile time. Native streaming support makes it ideal for real-time data feeds.
cards.gotcha: gRPC is not browser-friendly — browsers can't speak HTTP/2 framing directly. For browser clients, use gRPC-Web (requires a proxy) or REST. Use gRPC for backend-to-backend communication where you control both sides.

cards.title: GraphQL Trade-offs
cards.explanation: GraphQL provides a single endpoint where clients specify exactly which fields they need. This eliminates over-fetching (REST returning 50 fields when 3 are needed) and under-fetching (REST requiring multiple round trips). The schema is strongly typed and introspectable. However, it introduces complexity: N+1 query problems (solved by DataLoader batch loading), difficult HTTP caching (all POSTs), and a steeper learning curve than REST.
cards.whyItMatters: GraphQL shines for product APIs consumed by mobile or web clients that need flexibility over the response shape. Facebook, GitHub, Shopify, and Twitter all use GraphQL for their primary APIs for this reason.
cards.gotcha: Without query depth limiting and cost analysis, a malicious or careless client can craft a deeply nested query that exponentially multiplies DB calls. Always implement query complexity limits (e.g. graphql-java's MaxQueryComplexityInstrumentation) in production.
